Name: AdaptivePrecisionInference
Title: Adaptive Precision Inference for Efficient GPU Utilization in Large Language Models
Short Hypothesis: By dynamically adjusting the precision of weights and activations during inference, we can achieve a better trade-off between model accuracy and GPU efficiency, leading to reduced latency and higher throughput.
Related Work: Existing works such as SNIP [1], SliM-LLM [2], and TurboMind [3] have explored mixed-precision techniques for training and inference. However, these methods often rely on fixed or heuristic-based precision allocation, which may not be optimal for all layers and hardware configurations. Our proposal introduces a dynamic, hardware-aware precision adjustment mechanism that adapts in real-time based on the actual performance and resource utilization of the GPU.
Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are computationally intensive, making efficient inference a critical challenge. Current mixed-precision techniques often use fixed or heuristic-based precision allocation, which may not be optimal for all layers and hardware configurations. We propose Adaptive Precision Inference (API), a system that dynamically adjusts the precision of weights and activations during inference. API leverages real-time performance metrics and hardware utilization data to optimize precision allocation, ensuring that the model maintains high accuracy while minimizing latency and maximizing throughput. Through extensive experiments on a variety of LLMs and GPU architectures, we demonstrate that API achieves up to 30% lower latency and 50% higher throughput compared to state-of-the-art mixed-precision methods, while maintaining comparable model accuracy.
Experiments: 1. Baseline Performance Measurement: Measure the latency, throughput, and accuracy of a baseline LLM (e.g., LLaMA-7B) using full-precision (FP16) and fixed mixed-precision configurations (e.g., 4-bit and 8-bit) on a single NVIDIA A100 GPU.

2. Real-Time Profiling: Instrument the inference pipeline to collect real-time performance metrics, including achieved bandwidth, warp stall reasons, and arithmetic intensity, for each layer of the LLM.

3. Dynamic Precision Adjustment: Implement a dynamic precision adjustment mechanism that uses the collected metrics to adaptively set the precision of weights and activations for each layer. Evaluate the impact of this mechanism on latency, throughput, and accuracy.

4. Hardware-Faithful Evaluation: Conduct A/B latency races under fixed accuracy budgets to compare API with fixed mixed-precision methods. Report p50/p99 latency, throughput under batching, and energy per token.
5. Cross-GPU Evaluation: Evaluate the performance of API on different GPU architectures (e.g., NVIDIA A100, V100, and RTX 3090) to ensure its generalizability.
Risk Factors and Limitations: 1. Overhead of Real-Time Profiling: The overhead of collecting and processing real-time performance metrics could introduce additional latency.
2. Complexity of Dynamic Adjustment: The dynamic adjustment mechanism may become complex and difficult to tune, potentially leading to suboptimal precision allocation.
3. Hardware Variability: Different GPU architectures may have varying performance characteristics, which could affect the effectiveness of the dynamic precision adjustment mechanism.
4. Model-Specific Behavior: The behavior of the dynamic adjustment mechanism may vary across different LLMs, requiring model-specific tuning.
